coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode biosensor based on the catalytic activity of gold nanoparticles and magnetic upconversion nanoparticles for the detection of microRNAs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wu
Guo, Mengfan
Chen, Zeyu
Sun, Ying
Huang, Yudian
Chen, Yushan
Ma, Yanyan
Sun, Juan
Qiu, Bin
Lin, Hui
coredata.dc:description: The overexpression of microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) is closely associated with gastric cancer (GC) and may be used as a biomarker to diagnose GC early. Here, a colorimetry and fluorescence dual-mode biosensor for the detection of miRNA-21 was developed from polydopamine-coated magnetic upconversion nanoparticles (Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA) and aptamer-modified gold nanoparticles (AuNP-apt). The strong adsorption of single-stranded DNA by PDA results in close proximity between the energy donors (UCNPs) and acceptors (AuNPs), resulting in luminescence quenching of the UCNPs. The miRNA-21 can hybridize with the apt-conjugated AuNPs, and the AuNPs move away from the UCNPs, which results in the luminescence recovery of the UCNPs and the catalytic activity of the AuNPs. After H2O2 and tetrapaminophenylethylene were added, the yellow solution turned green and gradually deepened with increasing miRNA-21 concentration. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits of miRNA-21 by fluorescence and colorimetry assays are 8.90 pM and 185 pM, respectively. In addition, the developed approach was utilized to measure miRNA-21 in serum samples, and the recovery rates ranged from 105.4-107.1% and 98.5–109.3%, respectively. The above results indicate great promise for the early diagnosis of GC because of its high sensitivity and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polydopamine
Magnetic upconversion nanoparticle
Dual-mode detection
microRNA-21
Serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle and feasibility of miRNA-21 detection
Fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode determination of miRNA-21
Specificity of the proposed methods for miRNA-21
Detection of miRNA-21 in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of Fe3O4, (B) TEM image of Fe3O4@UCNP, (C) TEM image of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA, (D) EDS image of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA, (E) XRD patterns of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@UCNP and Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA, and (F) FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@UCNP and Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA.
XPS spectrum of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA + AuNP-apt: (A) total spectrum, (B) Fe 2p, (C) Au 4f, (D) C 1s, (E) N 1s, and (F) O 1s.
(A) Zeta potentials of Fe3O4@UCNP and Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA, (B) TGA curves of Fe3O4@UCNP and Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA, (C) water contact angles of Fe3O4@UCNP, and (D) water contact angles of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA.
(A) UCL spectra of Fe3O4@UCNP and Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA, (B) luminescence mechanism of UCNP, (C) magnetic response of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA, and (D) magnetization curve of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA.
(A) TEM image of AuNP, (B, C) particle size distributions of AuNP, (D, E) EDS spectra of AuNP, and (F) zeta potentials of AuNP and AuNP-apt.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of the detection system, (B) fluorescence spectrum of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA and UV–Vis absorption spectrum of AuNP-apt, (C) fluorescence lifetime of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA and Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA + AuNP-apt, (D) UV–Vis spectrum of TPE-4A in the presence of different substances, (E) FT-IR spectra of TPE-4A and TPE-4A2+, and (F) UV–Vis spectra of TPE-4A and TPE-4A2+.

(A) Influence of the AuNP-apt concentration on the fluorescence intensity, (B) influence of the AuNP-apt concentration on the fluorescence quenching efficiency, (C) influence of the reaction time on the fluorescence intensity, and (D) effect of the reaction time on the fluorescence recovery efficiency.
(A, B) Impact of pH on the UV–visible absorption intensity, and (C, D) influence of the catalytic time on the UV–visible absorption intensity.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra, (B) linear relationship at 654 nm, (C) UV–visible absorption spectra, and (D) linear relationship at 600 nm with various concentrations of miRNA-21.
(A) Fluorescence sensing specificity analysis, (B) colorimetric sensing specificity analysis.
Schematic design of the dual-mode measurement of miRNA-21.
Comparison of the performance of different methods for miRNA detection.
Determination of miRNA-21 in serum samples.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode biosensor based on the catalytic activity of gold nanoparticles and magnetic upconversion nanoparticles for the detection of microRNAs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Gastroenterology, Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University, 350009, Fuzhou, Fujian, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Gastroenterology, Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University, 350009 Fuzhou, Fujian, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pathology, Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University, 350009, Fuzhou, Fujian, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pathology, Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University, 350009 Fuzhou, Fujian, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a colorimetry and fluorescence dual-mode biosensor for the detection of miRNA-21 was developed from Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA and the catalytic activity of AuNPs. The LOD of miRNA-21 by fluorescence methods is 8.90 pM. In addition, the proposed method was used to measure miRNA-21 in serum samples, and the recoveries ranged from 98.5 to 109.3%, indicating broad application prospects in miRNA related healthcare fields.

The overexpression of microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) is closely associated with gastric cancer (GC) and may be used as a biomarker to diagnose GC early. Here, a colorimetry and fluorescence dual-mode biosensor for the detection of miRNA-21 was developed from polydopamine-coated magnetic upconversion nanoparticles (Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA) and aptamer-modified gold nanoparticles (AuNP-apt). The strong adsorption of single-stranded DNA by PDA results in close proximity between the energy donors (UCNPs) and acceptors (AuNPs), resulting in luminescence quenching of the UCNPs. The miRNA-21 can hybridize with the apt-conjugated AuNPs, and the AuNPs move away from the UCNPs, which results in the luminescence recovery of the UCNPs and the catalytic activity of the AuNPs. After H2O2 and tetrapaminophenylethylene were added, the yellow solution turned green and gradually deepened with increasing miRNA-21 concentration. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits of miRNA-21 by fluorescence and colorimetry assays are 8.90 pM and 185 pM, respectively. In addition, the developed approach was utilized to measure miRNA-21 in serum samples, and the recovery rates ranged from 105.4-107.1% and 98.5–109.3%, respectively. The above results indicate great promise for the early diagnosis of GC because of its high sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small noncoding RNAs that play important roles in regulating cellular activities such as growth, differentiation, development, and apoptosis [1]. Recent studies have shown that abnormal expression of miRNAs is tightly connected with many illnesses, such as gastric cancer [2]. Therefore, miRNAs are considered biomarkers for the initial diagnosis of gastric cancer. However, the extremely low abundance of miRNAs and complex biological environments require sensing platforms to be as sensitive and specific as possible. In recent years, several approaches involving fluorescence [3,4], electrochemistry [5,6], colorimetric methods [7,8], and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [9,10] have been proposed for measuring miRNAs. Among these testing assays, owing to their inherent merits of easy operation, high sensitivity and stability [11], the fluorescence method has been commonly used for detecting miRNAs [12]. In particular, fluorescence sensors based on organic fluorescent dyes [13] or quantum dots [14] provide many novel insights for biosensing, disease diagnosis, and therapeutics [15]. However, owing to the shortcomings of background self-fluorescence, biological toxicity, and a small Stokes shift, these methods are limited in practical applications.

Unlike traditional fluorescent materials, upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are a new type of luminescent material that can transform near-infrared light into UV–visible or ultraviolet light [16]. They have many merits, involving outstanding photochemical fixity, multiband emission and low poisonousness [17]. Owing to the excitation light being near-infrared light, most substances in organisms do not emit light under the excitation of near-infrared light, greatly reducing the light scattering of biological tissues, and self-background fluorescence can be neglected. Moreover, the reduction in light scattering caused by near-infrared excitation results in greater depth of penetration through biological tissues than that under ultraviolet or UV–visible light and can effectively reduce damage to organs and tissues within the organism [18]. Magnetic upconversion nanoparticles combine the easy separation and purification of magnetic nanoparticles and the unique properties of UCNPs, which have been used for the detection of pathogens [19], proteins [20], and mycotoxins [21] through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle [22]. To achieve a high fluorescence quantum yield and few nanocrystalline defects, UCNPs are commonly synthesized via the organic phase method, and their surfaces are hydrophobic because of the coverage of oleic acid. To be used in biological samples, hydrophobic UCNPs need to be converted into hydrophilic UCNPs. In addition, they also require appropriate functional groups for subsequent modifications. Therefore, various methods have been proposed to modify hydrophobic UCNPs [23]. Dopamine (DA) is subject to self-aggregation under alkaline conditions, leading to the formation of polydopamine (PDA) shells on different material surfaces. The PDA shell can enhance the hydrophilicity, stability, and biocompatibility of nanomaterials, thus attracting much attention in the surface modification of UCNPs [24]. Moreover, PDA can adsorb single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) through π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding, and other interactions [25]. Compared with ssDNA, PDA has a weaker adsorption capacity for double-stranded DNA. On the basis of this difference, FRET biosensors can be designed to achieve nucleic acid detection by using UCNP@PDA as efficient energy donors.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are generally regarded as effective energy acceptors in FRET bioprobes because their advantages include the tunability of optical properties, easy assembly with DNA, good biocompatibility, and high molar extinction coefficients [26]. In addition, multimode detection is important for improving the accuracy and sensitivity of the sensing system. Multimodal detection is mainly based on the combination of multiple signal responses from the same sample, including colorimetric methods, fluorescence methods, electrochemical methods, and surface-enhanced Raman scattering methods. It combines the advantages of various measurement assays to satisfy the demands of distinct environments and has complementarity and self- standardization effects to improve accuracy [27]. However, colorimetry and fluorescence dual-mode biosensors for the detection of miRNA have not yet been explored by using UCNP@PDA composites as donors and AuNPs as quenchers.

In this study, a colorimetry and fluorescence dual-mode biosensor for the detection of miRNA-21 was developed from polydopamine-coated magnetic upconversion nanoparticles (Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA) and aptamer-modified gold nanoparticles (AuNP-apt). Scheme 1 reveals the mechanism of the constructed bioprobe for detecting miRNA-21. Apt-modified AuNPs attach to the surface of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA through noncovalent interactions, and the red luminescence of the UCNPs is quenched by the AuNPs through FRET. After the target miRNA-21 is added, it binds to the apt, effectively shielding the bases in the DNA phosphate backbone, weakening the FRET effect, and inducing the departure of the AuNPs from the PDA surface, leading to luminescence recovery of the UCNPs. Therefore, miRNA-21 can be detected through fluorescence recovery of UCNPs. In addition, when AuNPs leave the UCNPs and undergo magnetic separation, the concentration of AuNPs in the solution increases. Under acidic conditions, AuNPs can catalyze the oxidation of tetrapaminophenylethylene (TPE-4A) to TPE-4A2+. Owing to their strong UV absorption at 600 nm, miRNAs can be detected via colorimetry.

FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, TmCl3·6H2O, GdCl3·6H2O, ErCl3·6H2O, YbCl3·6H2O, 1-octadecene (ODE), oleic acid (OA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonium fluoride (NH4F), ethanol, ammonia water (NH3·H2O), dopamine hydrochloride, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), phosphate buffer (PB), tris (2-carbonylethyl) phosphate hydrochloride (TCEP-HCl), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were bought from Aladdin Biochem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), carbinol, and cyclohexane were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Acetic acid, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrapaminophenylethylene (TPE-4A), and lgepal CO-520 were obtained from MACKIN (Shanghai, China). All oligonucleotides were bought from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).
miRNA-21: 5′-UAGCUUAUCAGACUGAUGUUGA-3′
miRNA222: 5́-AGCUACAUCUGGCUACUGGGU-3́
miRNA155: 5́-UUAAUGCUAAUCGUGAUAGGGGU-3́
miRNA-214: 5́-UGCCUGUCUACACUUGCUGCUGC-3′
Aptamer (apt): 5′- SH’-TCAACATCAGTCTGATAAGCTA-3′

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on an Ultima III X-ray diffractometer. The zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter were measured on a Nano-ZS90 potential analyzer. UV–visible spectra and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Lambda 950 ultraviolet spectrophotometer and a Nicolet-5700 FT-IR spectrometer, respectively. Upconversion luminescence spectra were recorded on a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer using an exterior 980 nm laser as the excitation source. The initial magnetization curve was tested on a type HH-15 vibrating sample magnetometer. Thermogravimetry was tested with a German Netzsch STA449-F5 synchronous thermal analyser. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a RapidXAFS 2MX-ray absorption fine structure spectrometer. The water contact angle was measured with a 209 contact angle tester.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared via coprecipitation. The preparation process was as follows: 25 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 15 g of FeSO4·7H2O were dissolved in 50 mL of water under nitrogen protection and heated to 80 °C, and 50 mL of NH3·H2O (28 wt%) and 4 g of oleic acid were rapidly added. The mixture was incubated at 80 °C for 3 h. The mixture was cooled, centrifuged to obtain Fe3O4 nanoparticles, washed 5 times with distilled water, collected with a magnet, and dissolved in cyclohexane for storage.

In reference [28,29], NaErF4:0.5 %Tm was prepared via a solvothermal approach. First, 0.02 mmol of TmCl3·6H2O and 3.98 mmol of ErCl3·6H2O, 24 mL of OA, and 30 mL of ODE were mixed, and the water was taken out at 110 °C for 10 min. Afterwards, the mixture was heated to 160 °C and held for 30 min. Then, 1 mL of cyclohexane solution involving Fe3O4 was infused, the mixture was heated to 80 °C to evaporate the cyclohexane, and then the mixture was cooled naturally to 50 °C. A total of 20 mL of methanol involving NH4F (16 mmol) and NaOH (10 mmol) was injected. The mixture was reacted at 40 °C for 30 min. After removing the methanol, the mixture was allowed to react at 300 °C for 1.5 h. Then, ethanol was added for sedimentation, the mixture was centrifuged to collect the nanoparticles, and the mixture was washed three times and kept in 20 mL of cyclohexane for later application.

First, 0.6 mmol of GdCl3·6H2O and 0.4 mmol of YbCl3·6H2O, 10.5 mL of OA and 10.5 mL of ODE were mixed and heated to 160 °C for 30 min. After cooling to 60 °C, 5 mL of cyclohexane solution comprising NaErF4:0.5 %Tm nucleated nanocrystal was added, and the mixture was heated to 80 °C to remove the solvent. Afterward, 2.5 mmol of NaOH and 4 mmol of NH4F were added and stirred for 30 min. Afterwards, the mixture was heated to 300 °C and allowed to react for 1.5 h. The obtained nanoparticles (Fe3O4@UCNP) were washed with cyclohexane and ethanol and redispersed in cyclohexane.

In reference to the literature [30,31], Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA was synthesized via the reverse microemulsion method. First, 0.65 mL of Igepal CO-520 and 10 mL of cyclohexane involving 10 mg of Fe3O4@UCNP were added. After stirring for 20 min, 100 µL of NH3·H2O (28 wt%) and 50 µL of dopamine hydrochloride aqueous solution (25 wt%) were added, and the mixture was allowed to react for 24 h. After the reaction was complete, the resulting product (Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA) was dispersed in PBS for subsequent use.

In accordance with previous methods [32], AuNPs were prepared via the sodium citrate reduction method. After 100 mL of HAuCl4 solution (0.01 wt%) was heated to boiling, 1 mL of sodium citrate solution (1 wt%) was quickly injected. When the color of the solution turned from light yellow to wine red, it was cooled to room temperature. 100 µL of the AuNPs dispersion, 15 µL of 100 µM apt, and 30 µL of 10 mM TCEP-HCl were mixed and kept for 2 h. Then, 2.5 mL of 2 M NaCl solution was added. Finally, the AuNP-apt was obtained by centrifuging for 20 min and then redispersing in 200 µL of PBS for backup.

The steps for determining miRNA-21 via the fluorescence method were as follows. To begin with, 20 µL of the Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA dispersion mixture and 40 µL of the 0.06 nM AuNP-apt dispersion mixture were mixed. Then, 10 µL of miRNA-21 solution at different concentrations (the final concentrations are 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 10, 15 and 25 nM) was injected, and the mixture was held for 2 h at 37 °C. Ultimately, the obtained solution was diluted to 300 µL with PBS buffer, after which its upconversion luminescence was determined.

After fluorescence detection, the above mixture was placed in a 0.4 T magnetic field for 20 min to perform magnetic separation, and the supernatant was collected for the detection of miRNA-21 via the colorimetric method. A total of 70 µL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (20 mM, pH 4), 30 µL of 10 mM TPE-4A and 10 µL of 1 M H2O2 were injected into the supernatant and held for 2 h, after which the absorbance spectra were collected.

Before detecting miRNA-21, the human serum sample was diluted 20 times with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4), and the standard addition method was applied to measure miRNA-21 in the actual sample and estimate the recovery efficiency. The detailed steps are expounded in section 2.5.

The synthesis procedure of the Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA composite is described in Scheme 1. The TEM image (Fig. 1
A) demonstrated that the resulting Fe3O4 nanoparticles were spherical with a mean particle size of 19.8 nm and an interplanar spacing of 0.25 nm, which was in accordance with the interplanar spacing of the (311) plane of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles. After being coated with the UCNP layer, the resulting composites had a hexagonal shape with a mean particle size of 72.7 nm, and almost every particle center had a prominent Fe3O4 core (Fig. 1 B), indicating successful growth of the UCNPs on the surface of the Fe3O4 nanospheres. After further wrapping with PDA, the mean particle size of the obtained composite increased to 74.7 nm, with an interplanar spacing of 0.53 nm (Fig. 1 C), which was compatible with the interplanar spacing of (110) in hexagonal NaErF4, suggesting that the layer of the PDA coating was approximately 2 nm. To confirm the composition of the prepared nanoparticles, elemental mapping analysis was conducted through energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). As displayed in Fig. 1 D, Fe was distributed mainly in the position of the nanoparticle nucleus, whereas Yb and Gb were distributed mainly in the shell position of the nanoparticle. The presence of C and N could also be observed in the outermost layer, suggesting that the three-layer core–shell structure of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA was obtained.

The crystal pattern of Fe3O4@UCNP was analyzed via X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 1 E). The diffraction peaks at 17.3°, 30.1°, 31.9°, 43.6° and 53.8° were in agreement with the hexagonal phase of NaErF4 (JCPDS NO. 28–1192). In addition, the diffraction peak at 35.5° (marked with a star) matched the cubic phase of Fe3O4 (JCPDS NO. 19–0629). As expected, polydopamine (PDA), an organic coating, did not have a significant effect on the XRD peak position or crystal form of Fe3O4@UCNP, and the change in peak intensity caused by PDA was not significant because of the thin coating. These results demonstrated that the main body of this core–shell structure was made up of UCNPs and Fe3O4. In addition, the chemical structure of the prepared composite was characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 1 F, the FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4 exhibits a distinct absorption peak at 563 cm−1, which is primarily attributed to the stretching vibration of Fe-O bonds. Fe3O4@UCNP had absorption peaks at 3448 cm−1 from the stretching vibration of –OH and at 2924 and 2854 cm−1 from the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –CH2 and at 1581 and 1463 cm−1 from the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –COO−, respectively. For Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA, the absorption peak intensity of –CH2 decreased, and peaks of N-H bending vibration and aromatic ring stretching vibration at 1633 cm−1 appeared, indicating successful encapsulation of the PDA shell.

The chemical states of the elements in the resulting Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA + AuNP-apt were studied via XPS, and the data in Fig. 2
A implied the existence of Fe, Au, C, N, and O. As shown in Fig. 2 B, there were two broad peaks in the high-resolution Fe 2p core energy level spectrum. The peaks at 724.48 eV and 711.84 eV corresponded to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2, respectively, which was in accordance with the standard XPS spectrum of Fe3O4. The peak at 718.78 eV was characteristic of Fe3+. The XPS spectrum of Au 4f (Fig. 2 C) shows two peaks at 83.65 eV and 87.41 eV, which are attributed to the 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 states of Au (0), respectively. The XPS spectrum of C 1s could be divided into three peaks (Fig. 2 D). The peak at 284.80 eV corresponded to C-C and C-H bonds, the peak at 286.38 eV corresponded to C-O bonds, and the peak at 289.67 eV was related to C=O bonds. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2 E) was deconvolved into two peaks, with the primary peak at 399.28 eV attributed to the amino nitrogen in polydopamine and the weaker peak at 403.88 eV related to the nitrogen atoms interacting with the π-π conjugated system. The peaks at 531.42 eV, 532.99 eV and 536.40 eV in the O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 F) were attributed to the Fe-O bond, the hydroxyl oxygen in polydopamine and the C-O single bond, respectively. All these characteristic peaks, as well as the TEM, XRD, and FT-IR results, clearly indicated that Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA was composed of Fe3O4, UCNP, and polydopamine, and was successfully prepared.

The PDA shell can not only improve the hydrophilicity of nanomaterials but also adsorb single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding. To verify the encapsulation of PDA, the potential of Fe3O4@UCNP before and after the encapsulation of PDA was first measured. Fig. 3
A shows that the zeta potential value of Fe3O4@UCNP was 25.1 mV. After coating with PDA, the zeta potential decreased to –23.5 mV, which may be due to the ionization of phenolic hydroxyl groups in polydopamine in aqueous solution. Fig. 3 B shows the thermogravimetric (TGA) curves of the Fe3O4@UCNP and Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA powders. Fe3O4@UCNP exhibited a mass loss of approximately 4.19 % in the temperature range of 170–500 °C, which was attributed to the thermal decomposition of oleic acid. In contrast, Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA demonstrated an additional mass loss of approximately 0.97 % due to the thermal degradation of polydopamine, indicating that the mass percentage of PDA in Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA was approximately 0.97 %. In addition, to investigate the impact of the PDA shell on the hydrophilicity of the magnetic upconversion nanomaterials, the water contact angles of Fe3O4@UCNP and Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA were also tested. Fig. 3 C shows that the water contact angle of Fe3O4@UCNP was high, indicating hydrophobicity, which was attributed to the OA molecules on the surface of UCNP. Fig. 3 D revealed that the water contact angle of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA was acute, indicating hydrophilicity, which was attributed to the presence of many polar groups, involving hydroxyl and amino groups, in PDA. Therefore, PDA can greatly improve the hydrophilicity of magnetic upconversion nanoparticles.

The luminescence properties of Fe3O4@UCNP and Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA were studied by recording emission spectra via a 980 nm laser. Fig. 4
A shows that both exhibited an upconversion luminescence (UCL) band at 654 nm, which corresponded to the transitions of Er from 4F9/2 to 4I15/2. Moreover, the absorption spectrum of PDA covered a part of the luminescence spectrum of the UCNPs at approximately 300–900 nm, leading to a slight decrease in the luminescence intensity of the peak at 654 nm after coating with PDA. In addition, the luminescence mechanism of UCNP is displayed in Fig. 4 B. Er3+ acted as an activator and sensitizer. The single near-infrared photons of the 4I11/2 state of Er3+ could be filled through ground state absorption and energy transfer from Yb3+ to Er3+, as Yb3+ had more absorption cross sections at 980 nm than Er3+. The NaErF4 host matrix promoted the capture of photons to the 3H5 energy level of Tm3+. Subsequently, reverse energy transfer occurred from the 3H5 level of Tm3+ to the 4I13/2 level of Er3+. During the upconversion process, near-infrared photons are further pumped to the 4F9/2 level of Er3+, resulting in red light emission at 654 nm. Finally, the Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA nanoparticles were dispersed in ethanol, and the magnetic properties were studied. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the nanoparticles accumulated around the magnet upon application of the magnetic field for 1 min. Moreover, the saturation magnetization value of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA was measured to be 54 emu/g via a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) (Fig. 4 D). The above results indicated that the prepared nanoparticles had superparamagnetism, magnetic separation, and reusability.

First, AuNPs were prepared via the citric acid reduction method, and their morphological features were characterized via TEM. The AuNPs were spherical (Fig. 5
A), with a mean particle size of approximately 9.74 nm (Fig. 5 B), which is much smaller than their hydrodynamic diameter (41 nm, Fig. 5 C). This may be due to the high surface energy of the AuNPs, which are prone to agglomeration and increase the hydrodynamic diameter. In addition, the EDS spectra (Fig. 5 D and Fig. 5 E) revealed that only the Au peak was present, and the synthesized nanoparticles contained 100 % Au, indicating the absence of impurities. Finally, the surface potential of the AuNPs was determined to be −10.2 mV (Fig. 5 F), which was attributed to the citrate ions adsorbed on the surface of the AuNPs. After the negatively charged adapter was modified onto the surface of the AuNPs, the negative charge increased, further suggesting that the adapter was modified on the surface of the AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and feasibility of miRNA-21 detection
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the feasibility of the developed assay for dual-mode measurement of miRNA-21, the upconversion luminescence spectra of different detection solutions were first tested. When the target miRNA-21 was absent, the red UCL of the UCNPs was quenched by the AuNP-apt through FRET due to the strong adsorption effect of PDA on ssDNA. After the target miRNA-21 was added, the AuNPs moved away from the PDA surface due to the specific recognition effect of the apt on the target, resulting in luminescence restoration of the UCNPs (Fig. 6
A). To investigate the mechanism by which AuNPs quench UCNPs fluorescence, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of AuNP-apt and the fluorescence spectrum of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA were first tested. Fig. 6 B shows the effective spectral overlap between the two, which was a prerequisite for fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence lifetimes of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA were subsequently investigated before and after the addition of AuNP-apt. Fig. 6 C shows that the average lifetime of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA was 105 µs in the absence of AuNP-apt, whereas in the presence of AuNP-apt, the mean lifespan of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA was 92 µs. This may be due to the differences in the donor lifetime induced by energy transfer between the energy donor and acceptor during the FRET process.

Furthermore, in the absence of the target miRNA-21, AuNP-apt in the solution was magnetically separated after magnetic isolation. However, when miRNA-21 was introduced, AuNP-apt remained in the solution without magnetic separation due to its detachment from Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA. Consequently, the AuNPs catalyzed the oxidation of TPE-4A to TPE-4A2+, resulting in a distinct UV–vis absorption peak at 600 nm. By using TPE-4A and H2O2 as substrates, the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA + AuNP-apt composite was investigated through the catalysis of their oxidation reactions. When Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA and AuNP-apt were coincubated for a period and magnetically separated, the collected solution exhibited no peroxidase-like activity (Fig. 6 D, red line). However, after coincubating Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA and AuNP-apt and subsequently adding miRNA, the magnetically separated solution demonstrated significant peroxidase-like activity (Fig. 6 D, black line), with a distinct absorption peak emerging at 600 nm. These results indicate that the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA + AuNP-apt nanozyme originates primarily from AuNP-apt, whereas Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA functions mainly as a magnetic separation agent and structural framework for supporting AuNP-apt. To verify the catalytic mechanism, we analyzed the reaction process via infrared spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 E. The FTIR spectrum of TPE-4A showed N–H stretching peaks of primary amines at 3424 cm−1 and 3354 cm−1, an aromatic C–H peak at 3025 cm−1, with 1619 cm−1 and 1510 cm−1 corresponding to C=C and benzene ring skeleton vibrations, respectively. After the catalytic oxidation of AuNP/H2O2, the N–H peak intensity of TPE-4A2+ significantly decreased, whereas a characteristic vibration peak of the azo group (N=N) emerged at 1416 cm−1, accompanied by shifts in the benzene ring skeleton vibration peaks. The UV spectrum (Fig. 6 F) further shows that TPE-4A2+ has a strong absorption peak at 600 nm, which is the same as the UV spectrum of TPE-4A catalyzed by AuNPs. These observations demonstrate that the developed sensing probe is suitable for dual-mode detection (colorimetric and fluorescent) of miRNA-21.

AuNPs have high fluorescence quenching efficiency, and PDA has different affinities for ssDNA and dsDNA. AuNP-apt can be adsorbed on the surface of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding, which quenches the fluorescence of the UCNPs via FRET. As the concentration of AuNP-apt increased, the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA decreased (Fig. 7
A). The concentration of AuNP-apt was selected as 0.06 nM, in which the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F and F0 express the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA at 654 nm with and without AuNP-apt, respectively) reached a plateau (Fig. 7 B). Next, under these conditions, we investigated the influence of the reaction time on the fluorescence recovery efficiency (F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@UCNP@PDA at 654 nm with and without miRNA-21, respectively). As displayed in Fig. 7 C, D, the time-dependence experiment revealed that the sensing system needed a reaction time of 140 min to reach recovery balance. Therefore, the reaction time of fluorescence recovery in the following tests was fixed at 140 min to guarantee complete recovery and achieve a stable signal.

Under acidic conditions, the AuNPs could catalyze the oxidation of TPE-4A to generate TPE-4A2+, which is a strong green absorbent species and has strong absorption at 600 nm. Therefore, the UV–visible absorbances of the detection solutions at various pH values were determined. Fig. 8
A and Fig. 8 B show that as the pH increased, the intensity of the UV–visible absorption also increased. When the pH was 4.0, the UV–visible absorption intensity reached its maximum value. As the pH continued to increase, the intensity of the UV–visible absorption in the detection system gradually decreased. This may be due to the pH-dependent oxidation-like activity of the AuNPs. Therefore, the pH was ultimately chosen as 4.0. Afterwards, the influence of the reaction time on the UV–visible absorption intensity was also studied. Fig. 8 C and Fig. 8 D show that with increasing catalytic time, the UV–visible absorption intensity of the detection solution progressively increased, reached its maximum value after 120 min, and remained stable. Therefore, the optimal catalytic time was set at 120 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode determination of miRNA-21
ce:section.ce:para:
On the basis of the optimal experimental conditions (AuNP-apt concentration of 0.06 nM, reaction time of 140 min), the upconversion luminescence response of the sensing probe to miRNA-21 content was studied by adding various concentrations of miRNA-21. Fig. 9
A shows that with increasing miRNA-21 content, the luminescence intensity of the detection solution gradually increased. Fig. 9 B shows a linear relationship between the UCL intensity at 654 nm and the miRNA concentration ranging from 1 to 15 nM. The linear equation can be denoted as Y = 0.09163 + 0.10147X (R2 = 0.99649). In the same way, under the optimal test conditions (pH 4.0, catalytic time of 120 min), the UV–visible absorbance of the detection solution was also tested at various concentrations of miRNA-21. Fig. 9 C shows that the UV–visible absorbance of the constructed biosensor at 600 nm increased with increasing miRNA-21 concentration. The absorbance was linearly related to the miRNA-21 concentration, which ranged from 5 to 25 nM (Fig. 9 D). The fitted linear equation can be expressed as Y = 0.00943 + 0.0159X (R2 = 0.99839). The limit of detection (LOD) is determined via the following formula: LOD = 3 × δ/S, where δ expresses the noise value, which is the standard deviation from 10 control samples, and S is the slope of the linear equation. The LOD of the fluorescence method was calculated to be 8.90 pM, which was lower than that of the other assays in Table 1 for measuring miRNA-21.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the proposed methods for miRNA-21
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition to high sensitivity, the successful measurement of miRNAs in complex biological samples also depends on the selectivity of the sensing platform. We selected three miRNAs (miRNA-155, miRNA-222 and miRNA-214) that may coexist with miRNA-21 in tumor cells as the interference group and increased their concentrations to four times greater than that of miRNA-21 (60 nM). Furthermore, to study the specificity of the designed probe for the target in the presence of multiple coexisting substances, three other miRNAs and the target miRNA-21 (15 nM) were mixed. As shown in Fig. 10
A, no obvious variations in fluorescence intensity were observed in the solutions of miRNA-155, miRNA-222, and miRNA-214 compared with the blank group without targets. The luminescence recovery rate caused by miRNA-21 was greater than that caused by the other miRNAs, and the UCL intensity of the mixture was close to that of miRNA-21, demonstrating excellent anti-interference ability.

For colorimetric detection, we also selected miRNA-155, miRNA-222, and miRNA-214 at a concentration of 80 nM as the interference group and investigated the determination performance of the constructed probe for the coexistence of the four miRNAs. As displayed in Fig. 10 B, the UV–visible absorbance of the interference group (at 600 nm) was not obviously different from that of the blank control group. The UV–visible absorption of the miRNA-21 (20 nM) solution was evidently greater than that of the interference group, and the UV–visible absorption of the mixture group was close to that of the miRNA-21 experimental group. In summary, the constructed fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode sensor has high specificity and anti-interference ability for the measurement of the target miRNA-21 in complex situations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of miRNA-21 in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm whether the detection platform can detect target substances in complex actual samples, serum spiked recovery tests were conducted. By adding a known concentration of miRNA-21 to the diluted serum, fluorescence and colorimetric detection were performed via the constructed probe. The concentration and detection results of the added miRNA-21 are shown in Table 2 . The concentration of the analyte substance was calculated through a linear equation, and the ratio of the concentration of the target substance to the concentration of the added target substance was the recovery rate of the sample. As shown in Table 2, the concentration of the analyte miRNA-21 detected by the fluorescence method and colorimetric method was similar to the concentration of the added analyte miRNA-21. The recovery rates detected by the fluorescence and colorimetric methods were within the ranges of 105.4–107.1% and 98.5–109.3%, respectively. In addition, the RSD values of the three parallel experiments were relatively small, suggesting that the constructed sensor has the potential to specifically determine targets in real samples.

A novel dual-mode probe for the measurement of miRNA-21 based on a single red UCNP was constructed by coupling the nanoenzyme activity of AuNPs and FRET. This developed approach has the following merits: (1) single red UCNPs emit light within the optical transmission area of tissues, leading to less scattering energy loss, thus enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of biological sample determination; (2) AuNPs can catalyze the conversion of TPE-4A to TPE-4A2+ at room temperature, resulting in excellent catalytic activity. Compared with natural enzymes, AuNPs are easy to prepare and highly stable; (3) fluorescence and colorimetric two-mode probes not only provide highly sensitive analysis, but also facilitate visual observation. The developed dual-mode probe offers a simple, fast, and sensitive approach for determining miRNA-21 and has prospective applications in miRNA-related healthcare fields.

Human serum samples were attained from Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University. Before the laboratory study, written informed consent was signed by all volunteers. All procedures were performed under the guidelines approved by Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University. The experimental procedure was approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University.

Wu Chen, Mengfan Guo, Juan Sun and Hui Lin designed research. Wu Chen, Mengfan Guo, Zeyu Chen, Ying Sun and Yudian Huang performed the experiments. Wu Chen, Mengfan Guo, Yushan Chen, Yanyan Ma and Bin Qiu analyzed data. All author wrote and revised the manuscript.

Wu Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Mengfan Guo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Zeyu Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation. Ying Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Yudian Huang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation. Yushan Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation. Yanyan Ma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation. Juan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation. Bin Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Hui Lin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Fuzhou Science and Technology Plan Project (2022-S-067), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2023J011492), key Clinical Specialty Discipline Construction Program of Fuzhou, Fujian, P.R.C (20230105).
